title: Witheringia ciliata
intro: Witheringia ciliata, also known as "Kola de caza," is a plant species native to the tropical regions of Central and South America, belonging to the family Solanaceae. It has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, cough, and respiratory problems, thanks to its expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties, which provide relief from congestion and soothe the respiratory tract. The bioactive constituents of Witheringia ciliata include alkaloids, such as solanine and scopolamine, as well as glycosides and flavonoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly prepared as an infusion or tincture, and is also used to make cough syrups and expectorant teas, making it a valuable resource in traditional herbal medicine.
taxonomy: Witheringia ciliata is a species of plant in the family Solanaceae. It belongs to the genus Witheringia, which is a group of flowering plants. The Solanaceae family is a large and diverse group of plants that includes nightshade, potatoes, and eggplants. This family is part of the order Solanales, which also includes other plant families such as Convolvulaceae and Montiniaceae. Witheringia ciliata is a specific type of flowering plant within the broader classification of Solanaceae.
